text: “Also, I think there has to be a better way to program computers than ascii text stored on disk” Love it! I’ve had similar thoughts as well
Will the Starship Enterprise be storing programs in ascii text on disk? Definitely not. It will be storing programs in _Unicode_ text on disk.
UTF8 Forever!  Mixed Reality – what fun!  At least we have one computing avenue that, though in various ways awful at the moment also has a certain giddiness to it!
Indeed it is. One of the things I’m working on is how to create interactive VR experiences from inside VR

You are 100% correct that APL's overly terse syntax which relied on special invented symbols, is hard to read. It was invented by a man far too smart for his own good, and blessed with a prodigious memory, he could hold in his head the precedence and left-to-right or right-to-left priority of all his operators. A more ordinary mind is constantly getting snared in APL's traps, where you don't understand how many operators are doing what in what direction.
Iverson did reform APL, and created a second try for his language, called J which is an improvement
Me too!  I've been enjoying the constraint: no text whatsoever!  Forces you to rely on space and movement.

I totally agree! I'd love to see a contest of "program visualization" where programmers / designers try to reimagine how code could look like if it wasn't restricted by syntax and a grid of monospace characters anymore.
I've argued similarly in another thread in this slack a while ago: <https://futureofcoding.slack.com/archives/C5T9GPWFL/p1559135414164600>

attachments.fallback:
[May 29th, 2019 6:10 AM] felix.kohlgrueber: Almost all text is ambiguous in some way and needs interpretation. Humans are really good at this (but not even close to perfect). Computers aren't good at dealing with ambiguities (and I'd argue they shouldn't have to deal with ambiguities at all). Because programs stored as text are ambiguous (e.g. "Which version of Python does this script require?", "Where do I find the library 'numpy'? And what version do I need?"), we invented a whole bunch of bolt-on tools (e.g. dependency management) and rules so that our programs become less ambiguous.

It's clear that these ambiguities need to be resolved, but we can choose when to do that. I'd argue that it should happen as soon as possible. For example, your editor should know that you write Python 3.7 by default and encode that in the storage representation unless you specify something else. The representation shared between people should be unambiguous.

Think of it like talking to a (human) assistant. If you tell them "Please call Mary and tell her that Tom wants to meet her at 8PM", the assistant would probably ask which 'Tom' you meant and would assume that the time was referring to your current time zone. Using the additional information, the assistant could inform Mary (assuming that 'Mary' is unambiguous). If they'd just do the call (without asking for clarification), Mary would have to guess the exact meaning of 'Tom' and the time.

In programming we're guessing and working around ambiguity issues all the time and I wonder if another storage representation could solve this.

Computers aren't good at dealing with ambiguities (and I'd argue they shouldn't have to deal with ambiguities at all). And what version do I need?"), we invented a whole bunch of bolt-on tools (e.g. dependency management) and rules so that our programs become less ambiguous.

It's clear that these ambiguities need to be resolved, but we can choose when to do that. I'd argue that it should happen as soon as possible. For example, your editor should know that you write Python 3.7 by default and encode that in the storage representation unless you specify something else. The representation shared between people should be unambiguous.

Using the additional information, the assistant could inform Mary (assuming that 'Mary' is unambiguous). In programming we're guessing and working around ambiguity issues all the time and I wonder if another storage representation could solve this.

text: I like the history lesson on typewriter tabstops, didn't know that. Do you know Elastic Tabstops (<http://nickgravgaard.com/elastic-tabstops/>)? It's a similar idea to what you're describing, isn't it?

text: There should be a joke in the next Star Trek film where a fancy UI fails and after a deep sigh the captain breaks out their command line
I made a second blog w/ a mockup of what just font and weight would do to help: <https://joshondesign.com/2014/08/25/typopl2>
ah. i didn’t know about J.
the other thing I’ve learned about APL and K is that it makes incredibly terse code for a certain class of problem domains, and in other domains it makes no sense to use it
Reminds me of the input font in some ways. They also have an example of combining different typefaces on their website. <http://input.fontbureau.com/info/>

attachments.title: Input: Fonts for Code — Info
attachments.text: Input is a typeface for code, designed by David Jonathan Ross and released by Font Bureau.

text:
Oh and this textbook also uses typography to increase program readability: 

Human factors and typography for more readable programs / Ronald M. Baecker ; Aaron Marcus

APL is not a domain specific language. To use it effectively, you need to learn how to encode your domain using APL data types and operations. For example, Aaron Hsu has created co-dfns, an optimizing compiler for APL that generates GPU code, in about 750 lines of code. The compiler itself runs on a GPU. My own GPU compiler is >16,000 lines of object oriented C++, with only a rudimentary optimizer. I would not have thought it possible to write this kind of software in APL, or to run a compiler on a GPU. The compactness of Aaron's code is astonishing. <https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=13797797>

J is easier to type on an ASCII keyboard, but I think it is harder to read than APL's ideograms. Now that Dyalog APL has incorporated many of J's innovations, I'm not sure that J is still an improvement over APL.

K has a seemingly richer set of data types than APL or J: records and atoms are a nice addition. The K data types are pretty close to JSON, so if you can encode your domain data in JSON, you can also encode them in K. I'm not actually an expert in APL/J/K, but I see a lot of potential in the basic design.

It feels like APL is good for things which can logically be expressed as operations on an array.  If your problem doesn’t fall into that sweet spot then you spend a lot of time shoehorning it into array-like thinking.  It sounds like K addressed that.

APL was decades ahead of all the other languages of its day. It was very close to what Mathematica became. It had a REPL terminal environment, and there was a built-in plot function that drew a nice graph (even on golf-ball terminal). Languages which have graphing built into the primitive system are wonderful for getting projects going fast. This is why Excel is so commonly used, and perhaps a very strong reason why Tableau got picked up for such a huge valuation. Drawing a nice graph is constantly needing to be done, and having such facilities already at hand makes for a much easier product acceptance. APL was actually a pain in the ass, and a few weeks after writing a paragraph of code it was like cryptography to read it again.
I didn’t know about APL’s plotting features.
That’s a really good point. The ability to quickly visualize things is very powerful.

I'm puzzled that you talk about "parsing" and then mention "disambiguation". In my mental model, parsing and semantic analysis are clearly separated; parsing does not imply disambiguation. I find it is often useful to write code that is both syntactically and semantically invalid; I can imagine a structured editor that keeps the syntax tree valid without getting in the programmer's way, but it's hard to imagine achieving that for semantics.

Command lines are one of my pet peeves. There may be nothing wrong with a stream of commands and responses per se, but how is it still based on fixed-width plain text?! I'd like to see it reformulated based on web standards ... but ideally, I think, not the real web with its enormous infrastructure and API surface, its reliance on JavaScript. Even that long-winded language where every punctuation mark has its own special name... &amp; &lt; &quot; &apos; &num;... it's a bit clunky. But I'd like to see a terminal based on a standard that was distilled from the most useful parts of the web - tree-structured (or better DAG-structured) information, the box model for display, styling, word wrapping, etc...

As I sit here reading this essay, various technical solutions come to mind for the problems presented. So why can't I work on implementing these solutions? One is obvious - an OS is under the control of the companies that make it. A third party cannot simply add a feature to Windows that allows two concurrent users with their own screens, mouse and keyboard, let alone a system where two users share the same screens; MS wouldn't want to redesign the OS to allow things like that because it might reduce their revenue (two users should buy two licenses!)

More subtly, I'm not a businessman, I'm a developer, and I have no interest in the business side of launching a startup. I have ideas that no businessperson could possibly have (extensibility and interoperability being technical problems), but without a business model and a business guy that can dream up a path to profitability and find investors, I can't implement my ideas. (UBI FTW?)

More broadly, while the business side is always a challenge, for a great many "future of coding" problems, it seems to me that the current world order is structurally against solving them (the incentives are missing, and we need those incentives to buy food). Which is not to say that the problems won't get solved, just that it will be a very slow, painful, annoying process that we will have to fight hard to be involved with.

Josh's blog on Why I'm a Climate Change Optimist: "Sure, we have had plenty of close calls, but somehow humanity managed to step back from the edge. In retrospect this is incredible."

Good luck in the past doesn't imply continued good luck. I am reminded of the anthropic principle: if we were dead, we wouldn't notice. Life may not exist in 99.99% of the universe, and where it arises it may die out in 99% of all cases. Whatever life is left over notices that it is still alive, but this is actually not a reason for optimism. (See also: The Great Filter)

Global warming is totally solvable and I like to talk to people about how to solve it... but I'm concerned because we're not on the right path yet.

I'd love to see what APL would look like if all its archaic and non-useful parts were refurbished with modern equivalents... as I have little time to memorize unusual syntax and semantics that don't help me solve problems, so I don't bother to learn about K or APL. I studied LISP briefly, but only enough to figure out how to add a lisp-style macro system on C# (see <http://ecsharp.net|ecsharp.net>).

"In fact our brains are so good at picking up patterns that we can read most English words with all of the letters scrambled as long as the first and last letters are correct." I dergasie. In retiliay ttah's icerocnrt. (I disagree. In reality that's incorrect.)

I was fortunate to speak with Iverson before he died. Although he was an incredibly brilliant man, it took a long time for him to realize that mere mortals had a lot of difficulty with his invented glyphs for APL, and he recast it in a new language called J which was his attempt to counterattack against Mathematica, which is the dominant math tool.
